attack_summary: An active ICMP flood (network-layer DDoS) was launched from a compromised Ubuntu host (192.168.1.3, elevated 'splunkd' agent) using hping3 to generate high-rate ICMP traffic to an external endpoint. The attacker remains live in-network; mitigations had to stop the flood without removing the host.

mitigations.name: Deploy Snort inline IPS (LAN-side)
mitigations.brief_description: Inserted a preconfigured Snort inline IPS between the FortiGate and the internal Cisco switch (FortiGate -> snort-ids-1 -> cisco-switch). Snort inspects and blocks ICMP-flood signatures inline without reconfiguring Snort or removing the host.

mitigations.name: FortiGate egress controls (deny/block)
mitigations.brief_description: Created a FortiGate address object for 192.168.1.3 and implemented a top-of-list firewall policy denying outbound traffic from that host. Initial attempts hit policy-ordering/CLI issues (block rule was initially placed after an allow-all), but the rule was re-created as the first policy to ensure enforcement.

mitigations.name: Endpoint network hardening (host-based ICMP rate-limit)
mitigations.brief_description: Applied host-side throttling on the Ubuntu host. nft attempts failed due to syntax/platform issues, so an iptables OUTPUT rule with limit (approx. 10/sec, burst 20) was added as a fallback to drop excessive outbound ICMP at the source.

key_observations: Layered defenses (inline IPS + perimeter firewall + host throttling) were used and proved effective: attack success rate dropped from 1.0 to 0.0.

Placement and ordering matter: Snort must be LAN-side (between firewall and switch) to inspect outbound traffic; FortiGate policies are evaluated top-down so correct rule ordering (or creating the deny as top policy) was required.
Host fallback is important: nft syntax issues required using iptables; endpoint controls provide source-side throttling and defense-in-depth.
Verification telemetry was recommended repeatedly — Snort alerts and FortiGate logs should be monitored to confirm blocking and to collect forensic artifacts before permanent containment.
All mitigations respected the live-response constraint: the compromised host remained on the network and was not quarantined, reimaged, or removed.
overall_conclusion: The team stopped the active ICMP flood while keeping the attacker in-network by combining an inline Snort IPS, an enforced top-of-list FortiGate deny-egress policy for the compromised host, and host-level rate-limiting. The mitigation was effective (attack success rate 0.0). Recommended follow-ups: enable FortiGate DoS/anomaly profiles and per-IP shapers, make host rules persistent and apply process allowlisting, and collect forensic evidence while monitoring Snort and firewall logs.
